top_profane.text:
As I am attempting to "green" up my lifestyle, I originally bought these to use for used cat litter and dirty diapers, but ended up replacing my seventh gen kitchen trash bags (temporarily) with these neat biodegradable bags. I love the idea of them breaking down, allowing my trash a chance to biodegrade faster.

These are a "whisper of a trash bag" and I had the most challenging task of using these during the prime summer months (Jun-Aug). My trash is picked up once a week (like most people's), and I experimented with these by emptying my cat's litter box (we use feline pine) in a bio bag. The litter had to sit 5 days before pick up, and I even kept the bag in a cool place. Wrong. The bag broke down and I had the unfortunate task of double bagging (that's where these bags would become outrageously unaffordable). Next, I changed my cat box changing schedule so that I would empty it on trash day (this experiment was in July). Still no good. My trash collectors come in the mid afternoon and the bag didn't make it through the hot humid day. From my experiments (at least in the summer months), I could not use these bags individually for feline pine cat litter.

Next, I used these bags for my diaper pail (still summer). I only have one toddler (almost 2) in diapers, and I found that the bag is fine if it is used for diapers (no wipes in the pail) and taken out weekly (which gets only halfway full in 7 days), but if I waited until I filled my diaper pail (which takes about 2 weeks), too much moisture got built up in the pail and the bag dissolved. Most people don't want stinky diapers kicking it in the house for 2 weeks, but it's unfortunate to use these expensive bags to half capacity, so either way, you lose.

Finally, I found a use for these bags, and that was as my main kitchen bag; but, you have to make significant changes in the way you throw out trash. First off, I read up on these bags, and a previous reviewer stated that if you put your wet trash in, say, an empty bread bag you were going to discard anyway, that these bags will work. I had saved a bunch of these kinds of bags for my cat's litter, so that's how I made these bio bags work. I'd line my can with the bio bag, and on top of the closed can, I'd have an old bagel bag or deli lunch meat ziplock as my wet trash reserve, and if I came up on wet trash I'd put it in that bag, then once that filled up I put the bag of wet trash in the bio bag. As long as the trash didn't get too heavy (or wet), my biobags would make it through a week. If you put wet trash (kitchen scraps, baby wipes, etc) in a biobag, you're doomed.

From my experience using the biobags, they really make you cognizant of how you throw things away. Sure, it seems pointless to put your trash in a bunch of basically plastic bags to put it in another bag, but at least you are taking one layer of plastic out of the equation. Every plastic bag I used to hold wet trash was going to be in my trash, anyway. You'd be surprised how many odd plastic bags you throw away (bread bags, empty chip bags, empty cereal bags, etc). It also showed me just how much wet, compostable garbage I make (kitchen scraps), and it proved to me how I really make enough fruit and veggie scraps for composting. But on the other hand, biobags turned me into a total neurotic over my trash can, as I played trash police with my family and friends. If you can get everyone around you to get with the "system," these bags will work for you. For me, honestly, it wasn't worth all the trouble. I am still sticking with my seventh generation trash bags. :(
top_profane.title: As a kitchen trash bag you MUST commit

top_profane.text:
I was initially attracted to the foot pedal placement. Being on the end would allow me to open the garbage without going around the side of my counter while still allowing me to keep the garbage in the same tight space in my kitchen that my previous can was located. It took some getting used to the extra weight of the lid and extra foot pressure needed to open it. Our previous garbage can lid was hinged on the long edge and was less weight to overcome than this garbage can lid. With the lid hinge on the narrow end, the effort required to overcome the weight of the long lid was almost double what I was used to with our old can. It took a few weeks, but now I don't even notice it anymore.

One thing i didn't account for was our counter getting in the way of the lid. Our previous can opened up towards the 2 inch counter overhang and was no issue. This can opens the long way and the lid hits the overhang preventing the lid from opening more than half way. My mistake, but easy to overlook if you plan on putting your garbage in a similar location to me. I had to put what would have been garbage, an empty cardboard box, in between garbage and cabinet as a spacer to push the can away from the cabinet wall enough for the lid to not hit the counter overhang.

Another thing i dislike is how slippery the base is on my floor. We have fake wood, but the surface has the same grip ability as tile, real wood, linoleum, etc. Basically the same surface slippiness as any kitchen floor. The base of this garbage can is a hard plastic so that the metal can isn't in direct contact with the floor. The hard plastic is VERY slippery on my floor, or any kitchen floor. The garbage can moves around DRASTICALLY with the slightest touch. Because of this, the foot pedal was basically useless because the garbage can just slipped out from under foot. I ended up having to buy some little adhesive cork circle things that are usually put on the bottom of chair legs. It still moves a bit but much much less than before. For the price of the garbage can, stabilizing rubber pieces should have been put on the bottom in the factory. The only floor that this wouldn't slide all over the place on is if it was on carpeting, but most kitchens aren't carpeted.

Taking the garbage can liner out to change the bag is more difficult than my previous can. Due to the hinge placement, you have to fully open the lid to vertical position in order to get the liner out and with the added weight of lid requiring more foot pressure coupled with the garbage can sliding all over, it is much more tedious to remove the liner. A small inconvenience for sure, but an inconvenience that my previous garbage didn't have.

Another dislike is the diameter of the liner is too small for any 13gal bag i have purchased. 75% of the time I end up tearing a hole in the final corner of the bag just trying to put it on the liner. It is marketed as a 12 gal trash can, so a 13gal bag should be able to fit. The next size up bag is just way too big for the garbage can.

Overall, i am "used to it" but dislike this garbage can. It looks pretty, but that's all it has going for it.

tl;dr
1. lid is heavier and requires more foot pressure to open
2. lid hits 2in counter overhang requiring a cardboard box spacer between it and counter
3. the can slides all over the place on my floor requiring me to purchase pads for it
4. the liner is slightly more difficult to remove
5. the 12gal liner is way too wide for a 13gal bag and the bag rips all the time

(the smaller office desk garbage can is amazing and the smaller scale removes all of the above issues with the kitchen can. The office desk can is awesome, no complaints)

I was very excited about this product before I ordered it. From the ad images, it looked like it was the solution to all the common problems I have when working by myself to fill the large, outdoor trash can liners with yard waste. It would stand the bag sturdily upright for filling from a full lawnmower bagging attachment, a leaf mulcher/leaf blower bag attachment, or for sweeping yard waste into the bag while lying flat on the ground. The solid internal walls created when standing a trash bag upright should also help reduce risk of tearing the bag with any tool used to crush yard waste down inside the trash bag by proving a smooth rigid surface to work against when extracting a tool. Without this surface on three sides, the tools used to do this often catch on the bag itself when being pulled out and rip holes. The top corners of the "wings" of this bag holder are rounded and smooth so that they do not catch and tear the bag when the trash bag holder is removed. (*Note, this product has a proper top and bottom: the top has the hand grip holes and they should be at the top when a trash bag is stood upright. Inserting the product upside down into a trash bag just makes it a little less easy to remove from a full trash bag. Be careful inserting the bad holder as the bottom corners are not rounded and can tear the bag if being carelessly inserted.)

When the product arrived, it came as a single, solid, flat, piece of plastic material. There are two "wings" connected to a larger single, flat, center piece that allow for some flexing back and forth. The wings are connected to the center piece along flexible, plastic-welded seams. When gently bent in the correct direction, the wings use their flexing seams to try to return to their original flat position and pull against the tensile strength of the trash bag material. Basically, the wings fold very easily in one direction and not easily in the other. The more difficult direction is the way to bend when inserting into a trash bag to create the rigid open bag shown in the ad images. The instructions enclosed state that you are supposed to fold the wings backward toward the face of the centerpiece with the image on it. This makes sense because this is opposite the direction of the natural fold along the seams and creates the tension that holds a flimsy trash bag rigidly open. In my case, the left side wing snapped off along the entire length of the centerpiece when I tried to fit the trash bag holder into the first trash bag of the day. It left a jagged edge along that seam which caused me issues later on.

Normally, I would have just scrapped the product, rated it accordingly, and then moved on with my life. But I was really trying to get a jump on this year's autumn leaves on that particular weekend and I had to press on and use the trash bag holder with only one wing. I needed to make progress that day and I could not wait for another delivery. I knew in doing this, a return would not be appropriate and I would have to settle with the $30+ partial solution. I have to admit that despite breaking as soon as I tried to use it, this product still was a big help to working by myself in the yard collecting and compressing mulched leaves from my lawn mower bagging attachment and my leaf blower/mulcher. Hence, the 3 star rating. I have to give credit where it is due and the unbroken seam on the unit I purchased held that entire weekend and the product still saved me lot of headaches working while by myself.

The primary drawbacks to using the the damaged product were that it
a) did not keep the bag taught and open all the way until the bag was at least half full, and
b) the jagged edge of the broken seam regularly tore holes in the bag when being puled out.

I have to move carefully when inserting or removing the bag holder from the bag to avoid catching the bag and creating tears. This is annoying but not a show stopper.

I am disappointed in the product having an immediate failure on one the wing seams the first time I tried to insert it into a trash bag, but it still saved me a lot of time overall. I used the extra large 40 gallon size trash bags too, so I don't think I was over-stressing the seams when trying to put the holder inside one. These particular bags may have just been taller to create the additional volume, so the failure on my larger capacity bags does NOT mean that this trash bag holder won't necessarily fit into the typical 30 gal black outdoor trash bags. It should work just fine. Just be careful when flexing against the plastic seams to insert into a trash bag or you might have a similar material failure to what I experienced.

I am not sure if I will purchase this product again. Perhaps a different brand. The concept is great and I'm sure it works when the product is undamaged but leaf season is not over where I live.Just not sure I want to take the risk again.
top_profane.title: DO NOT BEND THE WINGS TOO FAR BACKWARD!!!!

(1-31-2013) Like so many others, I will proclaim that oral irrigation is extremely beneficial to your health, blasting out rotting food particles, plaque and bacteria from places your toothbrush, floss and rinse can't go. If you only suspect you might need to try it, you do. If you don't suspect you might need to try it, you still do. Simply put, everybody should be doing this. It should be a standard and universal recommendation for every single person to include oral irrigation to their oral hygiene program alongside brushing and flossing.

I will NOT proclaim that Waterpik is the way to go for oral irrigation. Yes, I know, they've been around for years and set the standard and made it popular and Waterpik is a household name like Kleenex or Xerox, but their products are crap. Sorry, but it's true. They are not designed to last very long. I had the Waterpik WP=60W for a short while. The hose cracked after a few years of use. It is not easily user-replaceable. I have read the reviews of the replacement hose/wand kits and I am not impressed. It seems that the wand will not accept the assortment of older-style tips I have and I would need to "upgrade" to the newer style. That's a lot more money to buy new tips and a new hose/wand and why not just go all out and buy another, newer model Waterpik while I'm at it, it's only hard-earned money, right? I'm sure that's Waterpik's evil plan.

These days, it seems that just about every company is greedy as **** and wants to make a bundle off the customer with their poorly made products and when all the companies are doing that the customer base suffers dramatically, being nickle-and-dimed into the poor house. (For you old enough, remember when a box of Corn Flakes was actually affordable?) It all adds up. I want to save my hard-earned money and I can be rather crafty when I want to be, so I decided to figure out a way to replace the original hose with off-brand hose and splice it onto the original hose below the wand with tube connectors and save myself a bundle by not having to pay Waterpik for a new hose/wand replacement kit and replacement tips, so I pulled the base unit apart to get to where the original hose connects directly to the pump inside (I found a customer review that explained how easy that is to do). When I carefully tried to pull the hose off the nipple on the pump, the cheap, brittle plastic nipple broke with NO hope of repair or replacement or work-around. Looking at the inner workings of the base unit, I realized that these things simply aren't worth anywhere near the hard-earned money spent on them. They have virtually no user-replaceable parts and are built like a child's toy. I recall paying around $45 at Target for this piece of crap.

I researched the newer models of Waterpik and only read that the same problems still exist. The main problem being the hoses crack after a short while. If your product is registered they will provide you with free replacements, so that's nice of them, but why keep replacing crap parts with other crap parts? If the product is crap the product is crap therefore the product is CRAP. Is Waterpik really the best way to go? No. They are extremely noisy and messy and inconvenient and expensive and poorly made. FAIL, FAIL, FAIL, FAIL, FAIL!

Is Waterpik the only way to go? No. Time to move on.

I just ordered the ShowerBreeze w/ 6' hose by Oral Breeze which is inexpensive, convenient (you use it in the shower so no more mess to clean up!), quiet (no more pump to wake up the rest of the house!), efficient (doesn't require counter top space or electricity), made in America and is very well-reviewed.

These things are essentially a larger and beefier version of the standard plastic grocery bag, with some white powder in the bottom of the bag. The white powder combines with liquid (such as urine) to form a semi-solid gel. You then pull the bag out of the bucket, tie the handles together (pulling the knot down tight) and put it in the trash. Yes, it is convenient.

Something to watch out for: Compared to a commode without a bucket liner, it is far more difficult to tell whether it is time to change the bucket by just lifting up the commode seat and looking. Since the contents of these bags may NOT be flushed, it is extremely important to change the bag before the capacity of the powder to absorb liquid has been exceeded ... otherwise you could end up with liquid sewage in the bottom of your trash can, yecch. Product description says bags are generally good for three uses of commode--but I learned (ahem) the hard way that my patient, though normally lucid-minded, was not always reliable about telling me how many times she had used the potty and whether it needed to be changed. The solution I settled on was this: Instead of looking at the bucket to see if the bag needed changing, I lifted up the bucket and guess-timated how much it weighed. Product description says that the powder in each bag will absorb "up to" two quarts of liquid. I weighed a quart of water on my food scale and found that it weighed almost exactly two pounds. Thus, if the bucket contents feel like they weigh between two and (about) three pounds, it is probably time to change the bag.

Another thing to watch out for: If the commode is being used all the time, these bags really do add significantly to the volume of trash that your garbage disposal company will need to pick up each week. We had 20 gallon per week pickup, and the trash company requires that trash be in special carts that they provide ahead of time. My patient began using the commode the day after garbage pickup day. Within two days, I was wondering whether or not, by the end of the week, there would be room for the kitchen garbage, as well as the commode waste, in our trash cart! Had we used the commode all week, the commode liners alone would have taken up AT LEAST ten gallons of trash can space, perhaps more. Fortunately, I lucked out--my patient improved and started using the bathroom again before the end of the week, so we were able to get all of our trash picked up. But, extra trash volume is something to plan ahead for.

Then there is the factor of cost. If cost had been more of a barrier for me, this is what I would have done instead: Have the patient use the commode without the liners--but, place right next to the commode a small bathroom trash can lined with a plastic bag. Ask the patient to put their toilet paper in the lined trash can. The trash can liner could then periodically be tied up and disposed of in the trash--and the pee and poop in the bucket would be easier to rinse into the toilet and flush away, if not mixed with a whole bunch of toilet paper. (However, this does assume availability of a bathtub faucet that could be used to run rinse water into the bucket.)

Good luck to all you caregivers out there!
top_profane.title: Convenient, But Watch Out

I have to admit that I usually buy whatever trash bags are on sale - they're trash bags, after all, so as long as they don't fall apart before I get them out to the curb for pick-up, I'm happy. That said, there's no doubt that you get what you pay for. Generic and store brand trash bags are generally thinner and more likely to split. These Hefty SteelSak bags are definitely high end - they're very heavy, double-reinforced, and they hold a lot of trash. I was able to stuff four rooms full of trash (including my overflowing kitchen trash) into one of these, making for a very easy trash day.

I usually prefer the so-called "flap tie" trash bags, which seem less likely to tear than the drawstring ties, like these have. But the very heavy weight of the plastic eliminates that problem. These tie easily and securely, and the bags are easy to carry.

The big test for me, however, is how these bags fare with our crows. Crows are very smart birds, and they seem to know which days are trash pick-up days. They are usually waiting in the trees across the street from my house before I even put out my trash. They have no problem at all tearing apart those white kitchen bags and making a huge mess, and they've had some fun with some of the black trash bags I've used over the years. Since I don't have one of those huge plastic trash cans, I depend on heavier trash bags to protect my trash from the hungry crows. Well, these are great! The crows flew around them for a bit, but gave up pretty quickly and moved on to my neighbor's house. That's a big selling point for me!

Bottom line, these are great trash bags. They are more expensive than the bags I usually buy, but I figure they'll be on sale someday and I can stock up! They definitely live up to their name!

top_rant.text:
With inflation the highest since the 1980s, like any sane person who is trying to save money buying LACTAID milk, after reading reviews, and doing calculations (as I'm a math and science wiz), I bought these two items to test out and perform the math for you all.

My lactose BACKGROUND: In grade school through high school, I was able to drink milk just fine. Halfway through college, I started noticing bloating, gas, stomach cramps, and diarrhea after drinking milk. Fast forward a few decades and I can generally tolerate cheese and pizza, with occasional minor diarrhea a few hours later if I eat too much dairy. However, no cramps etc. If I drink milk though, even a few sips, or especially half a glass, I'll get painful cramps, and bad diarrhea. So I consider myself moderately lactose intolerant. My body or my gut bacteria must produce some minimal amount of lactase enzyme, otherwise I wouldn't be able to tolerate cheese.

===========================
*** ITEM: B007VTSMWG (Lactase Enzyme Lacteeze Drops 15.5 ml Liquid by Gelda Sci)

This item has 1,250 lactase units (LU) (or sometimes abbreviated FCC ALU or just ALU) per 5 drops, or 250 LU per 1 drop.

Cost: $19.50, or 6.29 cents per drop/250 LU.

It comes in 15.5 mL per bottle, so 15.5 mL x 20 drops/mL= 310 drops, therefore $19.50/310 drops = 6.29 cents per drop, or 6.29 cents per 250 LU.

There are 20 drops in 1 mL. This is scientific fact. End of discussion. (BTW, some products will falsely claim there's 25 drops per 1 mL. They are lying to you.)

To compare the first product with the second product, we must convert the cost to price per 1,000 LU to make the comparison equivalent. It's basic math at 250 LU x 4 (Lacteeze) = 1,000 LU (Kiddivit), so we can say this item is 6.29 cents x 4 = 25.16 cents per 1,000 LU (4 drops).

===========================
*** ITEM: B08DM7JDDK (Kiddivit Baby Lactase Liquid Drops 1000 Units - 100 Daily Servings, 1 Fl Oz (30 mL) - Built-in Dropper, Glass Bottle - Sugar Free, Gluten Free, Vegetarian Friendly)

This item has 1,000 lactase units (LU) per 1 drop.

Cost: $19.99, or 3.33 cents per drop (compared to 25.16 cents per drop for the Lacteeze).

It comes in 1 oz (or 30 mL) per bottle, or at least the label claims. That's almost twice the 15.5 mL of the other product, for almost the same price. There's a slight misleading claim here though, but it's minimal so I'll ignore it. What I mean is 1 oz capacity = 29.57 mL, and there's 20 drops in 29.57 mL, so that works out to be 20 drops/mL x 29.57 mL = 591 drops per bottle. The company's label on the bottle claims 600 drops. This is close to reality, but off by a few drops, which I'll ignore and give the company the benefit of the doubt.

$19.99/600 drops = 3.33 cents per drop.

Since this product claims 1,000 LU per drop, the price per LU is 3.33 cents per 1,000 LU. The Lacteeze product requires 4 drops to be equivalent to a single Kiddivit drop.

The Lacteeze drops (top item) is actually cheaper than other lactase enzyme products out there (trust me, I've done the math), but as you can see, this Kiddivit product is even cheaper than the top item, by about 1/8th the cost per LU, and an order of magnitude (10x+) cheaper than other products (i.e. probably 3.33 cents per 1,000 LU vs maybe 30-60 cents per 1,000 LU)!

In other words, you're paying 10-20x or more buying other products vs Kiddivit !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!111!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!1!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Mind blown.

Note: The reviews talk about a bad and slow dropper, so I completely removed the dropper and used a third party syringe thingy I can just stick into the bottle to get the enzyme to do the drops.

The product did not have a foul smell as some reviews noted. It also didn't spoil the milk in a few days, or hours.

===========================
===========================
TESTING CRITERIA:

1. I had two containers that can hold a little over 700 ml, so I accurately measured 700 ml of whole milk into these two containers.

2. I put 1 drop of the Kiddivit (item B08DM7JDDK) in one. This works out to be 1,000 LU per 700 mL. The Q&A says to put 1 drop in 1000 mL of milk.

3. I put 4 drops of the Lacteeze (item B007VTSMWG) in the other. This also works out to be 1,000 LU per 700 mL. The Q&A says to put 5 drops into 1,000 mL to reduce the lactose by about 70-80%, or 8-10 drops (to reduce the lactose by about 99%). For 1,000 LU/700 mL = [X] LU/1,000 mL, we solve for [X], which gives X = 1,428. 1,428/250 LU (per drop) = 5.71. This is equivalent to using about 6 drops of Lacteeze for 1,000 mL, 1 more drop than what the seller suggested.

4. The left over milk (1 gallon = 3,785 mL, and I used 1,400 mL already, so that leaves 2,385 mL), I used 19 drops of the Lacteeze on. This works out to be about 8 drops per 1,000 mL, or 4,750 LU per 2,385 mL (4,750/2,385 = 1.99 ratio, which is more than the 1.25 ratio (1,250 LU per 1,000 mL) recommended by Lacteeze at 5 drops), which should get the milk closer to 99% (probably like 90-95% lactose-free, I'm guestimating???).

NOTE: per legal regulations, in order to claim "lactose-free", the lactose content has to be below 0.1 gram per 100 gram of food, or basically below 0.1% (1/10th of 1%) of total weight. Milk has about 5% lactose by weight, depending on whole, skim, 1% etc. This means the lactose of 5/100 by weight must be reduced to 0.1/100. 0.1/5 = 2%. This means the lactose in milk must be reduced by at least 99% before products like LACTAID milk can claim "lactose-free". Per my understanding of lactase enzyme, if 1,250 LU (5 drops of Lacteeze) per 1,000 mL can get lactose in milk to be reduced by 70-80%, 8-10 drops, or basically double that, should get rid of 99% of lactose (depending on how long you let the enzyme work like 24-48 hours), making it equivalent to LACTAID milk. That means if you drink LACTAID milk and don't have issues, don't expect similar results by going only the bare minimum drops. You'd need to get closer to that 99% mark. I see a lot of people whining who didn't bother to do the math or research. Kwit yer whining! Plus every person's lactose tolerance level is different.

Per documented research, the lactose in milk takes about 24 hours to break down, so that's why you need to wait at least 24 hours, or more. Also, lactase enzyme works best around 98 degrees, which is the human body's temperature because humans evolved with lactase to help digest mom's milk as babies (duh!). But since you have to refrigerate milk, obviously that's colder temps so it takes at least 24 hours. If it gets too hot above 50 degrees C, the lactase enzyme protein starts expanding (unfolding) due to the heat. This makes it less effective at breaking down lactose. If it goes too high above 70 degrees C, like in hot chocolate etc., the effectiveness drops off the cliff. It's like a key, melted and no longer properly fitting the keyhole. Protein molecules and chemical reactions, especially enzymes work on the basis of protein folding (shapes). Cooling down the temps will only partially reverse this. Keeping lactase enzyme cold is required for storage and does nothing to the chemical structure. In fact, companies will add glycerin to lactase enzyme to extend the shelf life (probably to about 2 years, refrigerated???)

It's important to note that when I was using the Kiddivit baby product (item B08DM7JDDK), I noticed it was super concentrated vs the Lacteeze. I believe this is why the Kiddivit can claim 300% (4x as much - 1,000 LU vs 250 LU per drop) more FCC ALU than the Lacteeze per drop. The Kiddivit (meant for babies) has less glycerin and water content, and more lactase enzyme because mom's milk also has a higher lactose content (I recall 8% by weight) than cow's milk (5%). Plus I read somewhere that suggested the FDA regulating glycerin for babies, although I couldn't confirm the facts for this.

===========================
===========================
VERDICT:

1. After 24 hours, I tried the #4 step test (leftover milk (2,385 mL) with Lacteeze brand) first, which had an equivalent of 8 drops of Lacteeze per 1,000 mL, 3 more drops than the 5 the seller recommended. Everything seemed fine, no problems, no cramps etc.

2. I didn't want to try multiple treated milk to mess up my results, so I waited 36 hours (after initial mixing) to try the #2 step test (Kiddivit brand). Again, no problems, worked great. This is equivalent to just a little more than the 1 drop the seller recommends per 1,000 mL (as the milk was only 700 mL)

3. 48 hours (after initial mixing), I tried the #3 step test (Lacteeze brand), equivalent to almost 6 drops of Lacteeze into 1,000 mL. I ended up having a lot of gas later that evening. However, no cramps and no diarrhea.

===========================
===========================
CONCLUSIONS:

Overall, I had positive results, except for developing gas using the Lacteeze brand at about 6 drops per 1,000 mL, which is a little more than 1 quart. 1 quart = 946 mL.

The Kiddivit brand is much cheaper, at 3.33 cents per drop and per 1,000 FCC ALU. I estimate I'll need about 5-6 drops per gallon of milk. 1 gallon milk = 3,785 mL. I tested 1,000 LU (1 drop)/ 700 mL milk. For 1,000 LU/700 mL = [X] LU/3,785 mL, we solve for [X]. This works out to be [X] = 5,407 LU. 5,407 LU = 5.4 drops of Kiddivit brand per 1 gallon of milk. So if I use 6 drops per 1 gallon of milk, that works out to be 6 x 3.3 cents = 19.8 cents. That means for each gallon, I only spend 19.8 cents to make it lactose-tolerant for me, compared to $2-4 per gallon for other brands. Everyone's lactose tolerance level is different so you may need more or less.

The Kiddivit product comes in 1 oz or 30 mL the label claims. Since 1 oz capacity = 29.57 mL, and there's 20 drops in 29.57 mL, that works out to be 20 drops/mL x 29.57 mL = 591 drops per bottle. The company's label on the bottle claims 600 drops. This is close to reality. Using the lower figure, effectively, 1 bottle of Kiddivit can treat up to at least 591/6 = 98 (NINETY EIGHT) gallons of milk for me, at $19.99 total cost !!!!1!!!!!!!111!!!!

The Lacteeze brand is 6.29 cents per drop. Since I needed about 8 drops per 1,000 mL, this works out to be about 30 drops per gallon of milk. If I used less lactase enzyme at about 6 drops per 1,000 mL, it gave me a lot of gas. 30 drops x 6.29 cents = $1.89. This means if I bought milk for $5, I'd have to pay a total of $6.89 to make it lactose-free (for me). At 15.5 mL total, and 20 drops per mL, that works out to 310 drops per bottle. 310 drops/30 drops = 10 (TEN) gallons I can treat with Lacteeze brand at $19.50 vs the 98 (NINETY EIGHT) gallons I can with Kiddivit !!!!!11!!!!111!!!!!!1!!!!!

Again, Kiddivit is 3.33 cents per drop of 1,000 lactase units (LU) as compared to 25.16 cents per 4x drop with 1 drop costing 6.29 cents for the Lacteeze, but at only 250 LU per drop (hence you need 4 to make it 1,000 LU).

Note that many other lactase enzyme drops cost A LOT more than even Lacteeze, so you're probably looking at $3-$4 a gallon extra after you treat your lactose milk !!!!!111!!!!!!1!!!!11!!!

You'll need to look at or calculate the cost per 1,000 LU (or sometimes abbreviated as FCC ALU, or just ALU) to get true cost and not just base it on the drop counts, so you can compare real cost per enzyme amount. A super concentrated product like Kiddivit **AND** provides you double the amount (30 mL instead of 15.5 mL) but costs about the same is a no brainer.

LACTAID milk is 3 quarts (not a full gallon), at $6.24 for me in my local area. Per gallon, that works out to be $8.32! If you're paying almost as much, or more than LACTAID milk to treat your lactose milk, you need some math skills.

Remember when you (or a classmate) obnoxiously told your math teacher you'd never use what you were being taught in math class in real life??? EPIC FAIL! Learn basic math, or you'll be overpaying for LACTAID milk, or worse, go through all the hassle with lactase drops only to overpay even more. Or, you can give Kiddivit a try since I was nice enough to try it out and do all the math for you. ;)

You're welcome guys!
Math and science wiz, signing off

That means for each gallon, I only spend 19.8 cents to make it lactose-tolerant for me, compared to $2-4 per gallon for other brands. Everyone's lactose tolerance level is different so you may need more or less.

The Kiddivit product comes in 1 oz or 30 mL the label claims. Since 1 oz capacity = 29.57 mL, and there's 20 drops in 29.57 mL, that works out to be 20 drops/mL x 29.57 mL = 591 drops per bottle. The company's label on the bottle claims 600 drops. This is close to reality. Using the lower figure, effectively, 1 bottle of Kiddivit can treat up to at least 591/6 = 98 (NINETY EIGHT) gallons of milk for me, at $19.99 total cost !!!!1!!!!!!!111!!!!

The Lacteeze brand is 6.29 cents per drop. Since I needed about 8 drops per 1,000 mL, this works out to be about 30 drops per gallon of milk. If I used less lactase enzyme at about 6 drops per 1,000 mL, it gave me a lot of gas. 30 drops x 6.29 cents = $1.89. This means if I bought milk for $5, I'd have to pay a total of $6.89 to make it lactose-free (for me). At 15.5 mL total, and 20 drops per mL, that works out to 310 drops per bottle. 310 drops/30 drops = 10 (TEN) gallons I can treat with Lacteeze brand at $19.50 vs the 98 (NINETY EIGHT) gallons I can with Kiddivit !!!!!11!!!!111!!!!!!1!!!!!

Again, Kiddivit is 3.33 cents per drop of 1,000 lactase units (LU) as compared to 25.16 cents per 4x drop with 1 drop costing 6.29 cents for the Lacteeze, but at only 250 LU per drop (hence you need 4 to make it 1,000 LU).

Note that many other lactase enzyme drops cost A LOT more than even Lacteeze, so you're probably looking at $3-$4 a gallon extra after you treat your lactose milk !!!!!111!!!!!!1!!!!11!!!

You'll need to look at or calculate the cost per 1,000 LU (or sometimes abbreviated as FCC ALU, or just ALU) to get true cost and not just base it on the drop counts, so you can compare real cost per enzyme amount. A super concentrated product like Kiddivit **AND** provides you double the amount (30 mL instead of 15.5 mL) but costs about the same is a no brainer.

LACTAID milk is 3 quarts (not a full gallon), at $6.24 for me in my local area. Per gallon, that works out to be $8.32! If you're paying almost as much, or more than LACTAID milk to treat your lactose milk, you need some math skills.

Remember when you (or a classmate) obnoxiously told your math teacher you'd never use what you were being taught in math class in real life??? EPIC FAIL! Learn basic math, or you'll be overpaying for LACTAID milk, or worse, go through all the hassle with lactase drops only to overpay even more. Or, you can give Kiddivit a try since I was nice enough to try it out and do all the math for you. ;)

You're welcome guys!
Math and science wiz, signing off

Update Dec 27, 2022: The Lacteeze brand went up to $25, increasing the cost greatly. Kiddivit all the way for me baby.

Another thing that happened is I started astral traveling again! It hadn't happened for years! However, on the upside, we are more advanced medically and anatomically b/c we were all born with only 1 to 2 maximum wisdom teeth. None of the members of the family on my maternal side incl'g cousins and their children, grandchildren, etc... have more than 2 wisdom teeth. A few don't have any! The infant formula companies are upset because women are learning that breast milk is best milk so they are coming up with these funky capsules and telling you that your body doesn't make enough to care for your baby all so you will buy something from them and in hopes that it will put your mind at such a debate with yourself that you will forgo breastfeed and opt for their inferior products that make children sick and does nothing to build up their immune system because the only thing that can do that is your antibodies from the colostrum in your pre-milk, which is loaded with antibiodies that race through your child's system making their immune system stronger than it would ever be on fake milk by Enfamil and other companies! Your regular milk that comes in a few days to a week after birth is filled with antibodies as well though they are not as loaded as the colostrum they still continue to add to your infant's system making him or her stronger in the bones, muscles, tissues, eyes, motor skills, brain, etc... My youngest daughter who was breast-fed has held a 4.0 GPA since 1st grade and has been in the AIG program (academically & intellectually gifted program) since kindergarten! Her older sister wasn't the brain her little sister is and she was sick all the time when she was born b/c of the fake milk from Enfamil, which I feel so responsible for doing to her but there were no breast-milk banks to buy from in those days no matter how much I wish there were; however, she had ADD as well but with care, an excellent vegetarian diet, plenty of exercise, studying, psychotherapy to help with the ADD, she held a B average from 1st grade through senior year when she graduated and she earned an associate degree along with her state board certification license allowing her to work and showing her as a CNA (Certified Nursing Assistance) - all 2 weeks before her commencement ceremony, where it was mentioned and she received a special certification from the high school for earning her associate degree and passing her state board exam earning her practitioner's license all before graduating high school! You can turn it around but why would you want to have to put so much into caring for your child on formula when you can go the easy route and breast feed?

Think about it: There's no need for any boiling for sterilization, no heating bottles in hot water (microwaves are not recommended for heating ANY MILK, BREAST MILK, yes it can be pumped & frozen for use later; and FAKE MILK), no need for washing bottles. Your baby's diaper bag is 10-tons lighter because there is no need for bottles, spit-up rags by the dozen because fake-milk fed babies spit up a truck-load of the milk b/c it is not easily digested by their bodies! Breast milk causes very little to no spit up and there is little to no air after you learn to help your baby latch onto the breast properly. top_rant.text:
(I've ordered a bunch of candles from this company & I have ZERO complaints about any of them, so I'm going to copy & paste the same review of mine for each of their candles since it really states ALL of the wonderful things about these candles!!) *Please read on!* :)

**And I just noticed on the back of their candle boxes it says "Your purchase plants one tree through our partnership with One Tree Planted." How awesome is that! AND it also states their wood wicks are from "FSC Certified wood mills" meaning that the wood is sustainably harvested. Big Kudos to this candle company for both of those environmentally-conscious efforts!! :D

I really LOVE candles from this company & I can't rave about them enough! I'm so glad they're made in the U.S. & I LOVE that they're made with coconut wax & essential oils & NO chemical fragrances! And the wood wick is a neat & cool addition to it. It's packaged beautifully & in a very classy & elegant way, which is also suitable for gift giving to either men or women. :) I have only a TON of good things to say about their candles if you care to read on & please do!! :D

This Oud Forest candle has a really pleasing, "nice & cozy" frankincense, fruity, floral, woodsy sort of scent & it fills up my living room, dining room, & kitchen with its scent quickly, but isn't too overpowering where you feel "suffocated" by a "strong, saturated" scent like some chemically-scented candles out there can do. So I really appreciate this is an ALL NATURAL candle made with ONLY coconut wax & essential oils & NO chemical fragrances! It's much better/safer for me & my family & our pets! :)

However, I have to be honest & say this is the ONLY candle out of the MANY candles I've tried from this company so far that seems to have a bit of an allergic effect on me...the inside of my nostrils started to get a bit bothered by it shortly after lighting it & I started to sneeze a little & I was really surprised since it's such a natural candle with no chemical fragrances in it & I've had NO problem with any of their other candle scents so far! (And I have a lot of their candles & I've lit some of them for hours at a time with NO issues whatsoever!) :)

So I decided to look up what "oud" is online...& it says that it's the resin/sap from a tree where the tree creates this special "oud" sap in response to a mold infestation in the tree. So I believe that perhaps that could be why only this particular scent bothers me a little bit. I do get seasonal allergies in the Spring & Fall and I have some food allergies as well, so perhaps when they're extracting this "oud" sap from the trees, maybe some of the microscopic mold spores I imagine may wind up in the sap that gets turned into essential oil...which goes into fragrancing this candle...& so I guess I must be sensitive/somewhat allergic to that...just like during Spring & Fall allergy seasons. Bummer! :(

So that's my only caution with this particular scent...that if someone has seasonal allergies, food allergies, asthma, or breathing issues of any sort, perhaps just steer clear of this particular scent, or use it outdoors. That's what I think I'm going to do...is just light this one for guests to enjoy outdoors on my porch so it won't bother me as much then probably, where it's "airing" out! :)

But ALL of the other candle scents I've tried from this company haven't bothered me at all & I usually don't have "allergic" reactions to any other candles either! It may just be one particular ingredient in this candle...perhaps the "oud" scent in it. But it is a very good smelling candle!! :D

The wood wick is VERY effective too!...It's MUCH better than regular wicks, I think! The scent starts to emanate out from the candle pretty quickly after lighting it & it burns very evenly...much better than regular wicks in my opinion, especially in a large jar. I've had NO trouble with this wick/candle creating that unwanted "tunneling" effect where the outer wax of the candle doesn't burn & you wind up with a hole burning down into the center of the wax, thereby wasting the entire outer wax of the candle. This candle DOESN'T do that, as long as your burn it for at least an hour or so each time. There's a little instruction card included in the box that suggests this...to let your candle burn for about 2 hours each time if possible, & not for longer than 4 hours, to create a nice, even melting of your wax & no tunneling hole!

I also really appreciate that these are MADE IN THE USA!! Thank goodness...so few products are made in America anymore, so I wholeheartedly enjoy supporting American companies & American-made products & American employees/families! :) :) :) So it's just another thing to feel good about with this candle!

And I also really like that it's made with COCONUT wax & not soy wax, which even though soy wax is a "natural vegetable wax" it's still most likely going to be made from genetically-modified soy (unless a candle specifically says it's made with "organic" soy wax.) So soy wax candles are really just contributing to the whole GMO problem (but at least they're better than paraffin (petroleum-derived) candle wax.) This candle being made of coconut wax though, is even better than soy wax, & so it's just another big plus in my book for this company's candles! :D

And it must be true what they say about coconut wax burning longer since these coconut wax candles burn WAY longer than ANY candle I've ever had! I've had one of these same candles from this company in a different scent that I've burned about 5 times already for a total of at least 15 hours so far, & the wax has hardly burned down at all! Seriously! It's amazing!! You truly get your money's worth out of these candles!! $$$ The wax lasts a LONG time!

People can say these are "expensive" candles...but you get what you pay for...& I feel these are worth the price if you can afford it, since mine are really lasting a LONG time...they don't burn up quickly like cheap candles can do. And the candle still gives off a lot of pleasant scent without having to burn/waste a lot of the wax. I think the wood wick really controls how slowly the wax burns too, so the wax isn't getting burned up quickly. So while you may pay more for this candle than other candles, it lasts a LOT LONGER than other candles do!

I prefer to use essential oil scented candles too & to pay more for them when I can afford to, rather than using chemically-scented candles all the time. Chemical fragrances are one of the most unregulated industries out there & people don't realize how toxic "fragrances" can be. I feel chemical fragrances are perhaps really contributing to the allergy problem that's been growing in recent years. It's completely up to the discretion of a company to use whatever chemicals they wish to make fragrances with & in whatever strong concentration they choose, & everything is becoming so powerfully scented that all of these chemical scents are becoming overwhelming & bombarding our senses & bodies on a daily basis (think about laundry detergent, perfumes, etc.) The safety & toxicity of all of these fragrances aren't really regulated by the government or any organization either. We're overwhelming our noses & respiratory systems with chemical fragrances on a daily basis & then we wonder why people are breaking out in overwhelming numbers with allergies, skin issues, asthma, breathing problems, etc. So I'm all for companies like this one who use only essential oils for their fragrances in their product instead of using unnecessary, unhealthy chemical scents!! :D *THANK YOU!*

And the wood wick is in my opinion a neat, cool effect that my son & I enjoy looking at the candle & listening to! Sure, it doesn't sound like a roaring fire lol, & you aren't going to "hear" it unless you get up close to the candle & listen to it or if you're sitting in quiet room with it, but my son & I thoroughly enjoy the effect of it. It's nice to just sit & listen to it for a few minutes with him & have some peace & quiet enjoying it...teaching my kid that not everything has to be "loud & fun" to enjoy something in life! And if you're quietly reading or using your computer with no tv/music on in the background, you can hear the quiet, relaxing crackling of this candle! :P

The packaging the candle comes in is really, really nice for gift giving too! It comes in an elegant, well made black box with a flip-open lid that easily & conveniently magnetizes shut & is really great for re-using! Inside of it the candle is snugly wrapped in a black velvet bag (my son just loves those types of bags & he uses them as "coin purses" like he's a "pirate" lol!) The glass jar is great for re-using as well & I like that the design on it is simple & classy & isn't a big sticker label like other candles have that wind up looking tacky sometimes if you want to re-use the empty jar for anything when the candle is done with. And then if you wash the jar more than once, those stickers can wind up starting to peel off...but with this jar you don't have that kind of problem! :)

The amber/brown jar also lights up nicely when the candle is lit & I like that it's a pleasing color to men as well, so you can make the man in your life happy to use these candles as well! This company's candles make great gifts not only for women, but also for men, being that they aren't packaged in any kind of "girly" way & many of the scents I think are pleasing to both men & women!

It also came with an additional little candle in a black metal tin. It's "Palo Santo" scent for you to try (which is one of my favorite scents from this company...a yummy frankincense-clove sort of scent!) And the lid screws on, making it a secure little tin to hold jewelry in while traveling, etc...once the candle is used up & you wash it out.

Overall, I think this is another one of this company's WONDERFUL candles & it's nicely scented for gift giving or for treating yourself! It really hits all of the boxes for me for what I'd want in a candle...being made in the USA & made of coconut wax & essential oils & having no chemical fragrances, in a nice amber glass jar with no sticker label on it, packaged in a lovely, elegant way with other usable items (the fancy black box & black velvet pouch)...which makes it very suitable for gift giving to a man, not just to a woman...being that it isn't packaged or decorated in a distinctly "feminine" sort of way. And the company makes various "woodsy" scents as well, which any man would be sure to appreciate! And then it also comes with an extra little candle yet too!

I think this is just a superb product that goes even above & beyond your usual "natural candle." So a customer can feel good supporting an American company like this which produces such a quality, well made, nicely-crafted, all-natural, eco-friendly item! And don't forget that they've partnered with a tree-planting organization that plants one tree for every candle of theirs purchased, so any candle you purchase from them also pays for a tree being planted! Which is SO IMPORTANT nowadays with how big a problem deforestation has become! So what isn't there to like about this company & their candles?! Even their wood wicks are from FSC-certified wood mills which means the wood is supposed to be sustainably harvested! :D I wish every company was this conscientious with the products they made!
top_rant.title: LOVE this company's candles!! BEST candles I've ever had! All natural & made in the USA! Great gift!

top_rant.text:
Some background:
I am an experienced cup user who has tried several different cups (Lunette 2, lily cup compact B, Saalt cup regular, Lena large, and now femmycycle regular).
I have had three natural births.
I have a high cervix during my period.

I never experienced much of a learning curve since I started using cups. My first cup was a lunette size 2 and I popped it in, it opened, and it did what it was meant to do. Same with the saalt cup and lily compact (although lily compact is harder to seal and get open bc it is a very soft cup). The Lena cup (the regular, not sensitive) caused some bladder and urethra pain. As soon as I’d put it in, I’d get a feeling I’d have to pee, bad, even though I just peed. Then I would try to relieve myself and nothing would come out, or very little.

I like the idea of a cup that had a no spill rim (even though I didn’t find cups particularly any more messy than a pad or tampon), and I liked that it didn’t have to be open all the way! With that said...

It had been delivered in a timely fashion before I expected it, which was nice considering my period came a whole 5 days early, the day after my cup came. YES!! I was on my toilet trying to insert this thing with my 18 month old screaming about Elmo bandaids. Not a good time. I started with the c fold (or U fold) and the cup literally stayed in that shape inside of me. I did what the instructions said and inserted the cup and as soon as the ring right inside the entrance to my vagina, I let it go. So I removed it and tried the punch down fold. I know the body of the cup doesn’t have to be opened all way to work, but the rim needs to be open. So I tried again and after some maneuvering, I thought it was open. I waited a couple of hours, took a shower and removed the femmycycle, literally nothing was in the cup and it all came pouring out of me after the cup was out. So it wasn’t working.

Okay round 2. I usually insert my cups on the toilet, but figured I’d try with one leg on the tub since I was in the shower. Every freaking time the cup just stayed folded in whichever way I inserted it. Didn’t matter what fold, 7-fold, punch down, tulip, c fold. I watched video after video and I did everything that was recommended. Finally I felt the rim pop (or so I thought) and I sat on the couch and watched TV. I went to check for leaks and I didn’t see anything in my period underwear,l but when I wiped I saw some pink, I assumed it was just what was on the walls of my vagina, since that can happen. I went up to bed. I woke up at 12:30 am to a gush (4 hours after insertion) and again, no blood inside the cup at all, not a drop, and blood all over my hands from removal and it all poured out after the cup again (FYI if you have a cup that fits well, and creates a good seal, you should NOT get blood on your hands bc it is all contained in the cup). So I honestly washed the femmycycle, put it away, popped in my Lunette cup and went back to bed.

I felt like a failure though. I have made every other cup I’ve tried work with minimal effort. What the heck was I doing wrong? Not to mention I was pissed that the femmycycle is a lot more expensive than other name brand cups.

The next day I had the day off so I figured I’d give it another shot. After I dropped the kids off to school and daycare, I went home and watched videos and read and posted on a menstrual cup FB group. I got a lot of advice and tips but honestly, none worked. I tried lube, I tried squaring, I tried laying on the floor, I tried every fold I could think of and even made some up, I tried everything but doing a headstand and bathing in lube (although I was willing to try anything if someone told me it worked).

One woman (bless her heart for she is the savior of my femmycycle) told me that she had the same problems and instead of inserting the cup just until it is right inside the vaginal opening, try pushing it up higher. So now I’m on try number 473 and am obviously expecting a failure again, bc obviously my vagina isn’t built for the femmycycle amirite?

OMG!!!!! It worked!!! I popped open and I could feel it pop open! I know the body of the cup can be somewhat collapsed so I didn’t worry about that, but the rim was definitely open! I still didn’t trust it completely bc, like I said before, I had thought it was open before and no blood had gotten in the cup. I checked the cup every so often and no leaks! No pink on the paper! No blood on my hands! So I went to bed. When I woke up at 6am the following day my first thought was “I better not feel a gush when I stand” and I didn’t!!!! I skipped to the bathroom, washed my hands, inserted a finger, found the loop, pushed with my pelvic floor muscles (like pooping or giving birth), then when the cup was at the vaginal opening I squeezed the base and removed. Guys!!!! The cup was FULL! It worked!!!! It worked and it was so comfortable. I didn’t feel a thing, I didn’t feel like I had to pee all night and I know a lot of woman say the suction is super strong, but it came out fairly easily for me. I didn’t notice the suction was any stronger than my Lunette cup.

So if your having the same issue as me, please try this!!
- Punch down fold (use a little lube on the insertion point, I do this sometimes towards the end of my period when things aren’t as naturally lubricated).

-Insert as you normally would and hold the cup until it is as high as you can get it.

- grab a hold of the removal ring and pull down a little (this should get the rim open).

- Push the cup back up.

- Done!!!

REMOVAL

- use whatever position you are comfy with. Some woman like to squat. I highly suggest this if you are like me and have a higher cervix and the cup rides up over night and is difficult to reach.

-insert a finger and get it around the loop. Gently walk the cup down while using your muscles to guide the cup down.

-when low enough, pinch the base of the cup to break the seal and remove!

-Rinse and repeat.

I have a pretty average flow and can always go 10-12 hours with this cup, although that is normal for me with any cup.
The removal ring is sooo convenient! If you are like me and have a high cervix, the rim is super convenient! The cup body is super soft and comfortable. I don’t feel a thing! Most cups I feel something, even if that something isn’t a big deal. It is awesome that I can insert the cup and not have to make sure it isn’t collapsed. Makes things much quicker in the bathroom. Since learning how to make the femmycycle work for me, I haven’t had any leaks at all and don’t need to use a pantyliner.

I will say, even as someone who has experience with cups, this cup is very different and bad its own learning curve and can be a steep one. DON’T GIVE UP! Once you find what works for you, this cup is amazing and so easy to use!!!

TL;DR- Femmycycle is amazing although it has its own learning curve. Was about to throw it away and so glad I didn’t!
top_rant.title: Hands down, femmycycle rules them all!

So I really appreciate this is an ALL NATURAL candle made with ONLY coconut wax & essential oils & NO chemical fragrances! It's much better/safer for me & my family & our pets! :)

The wood wick is VERY effective too!...It's MUCH better than regular wicks, I think! The scent starts to emanate out from the candle pretty quickly after lighting it & it burns very evenly...much better than regular wicks in my opinion, especially in a large jar. I've had NO trouble with this wick/candle creating that unwanted "tunneling" effect where the outer wax of the candle doesn't burn & you wind up with a hole burning down into the center of the wax, thereby wasting the entire outer wax of the candle. This candle DOESN'T do that, as long as your burn it for at least an hour or so each time. There's a little instruction card included in the box that suggests this...to let your candle burn for about 2 hours each time if possible, & not for longer than 4 hours, to create a nice, even melting of your wax & no tunneling hole!

I also really appreciate that these are MADE IN THE USA!! Thank goodness...so few products are made in America anymore, so I wholeheartedly enjoy supporting American companies & American-made products & American employees/families! :) :) :) So it's just another thing to feel good about with this candle!

And I also really like that it's made with COCONUT wax & not soy wax, which even though soy wax is a "natural vegetable wax" it's still most likely going to be made from genetically-modified soy (unless a candle specifically says it's made with "organic" soy wax.) So soy wax candles are really just contributing to the whole GMO problem (but at least they're better than paraffin (petroleum-derived) candle wax.) This candle being made of coconut wax though, is even better than soy wax, & so it's just another big plus in my book for this company's candles! :D

And it must be true what they say about coconut wax burning longer since these coconut wax candles burn WAY longer than ANY candle I've ever had! I've had one of these same candles from this company in a different scent that I've burned about 5 times already for a total of at least 15 hours so far, & the wax has hardly burned down at all! Seriously! It's amazing!! You truly get your money's worth out of these candles!! $$$ The wax lasts a LONG time!

People can say these are "expensive" candles...but you get what you pay for...& I feel these are worth the price if you can afford it, since mine are really lasting a LONG time...they don't burn up quickly like cheap candles can do. And the candle still gives off a lot of pleasant scent without having to burn/waste a lot of the wax. I think the wood wick really controls how slowly the wax burns too, so the wax isn't getting burned up quickly. So while you may pay more for this candle than other candles, it lasts a LOT LONGER than other candles do!

I prefer to use essential oil scented candles too & to pay more for them when I can afford to, rather than using chemically-scented candles all the time. Chemical fragrances are one of the most unregulated industries out there & people don't realize how toxic "fragrances" can be. I feel chemical fragrances are perhaps really contributing to the allergy problem that's been growing in recent years. It's completely up to the discretion of a company to use whatever chemicals they wish to make fragrances with & in whatever strong concentration they choose, & everything is becoming so powerfully scented that all of these chemical scents are becoming overwhelming & bombarding our senses & bodies on a daily basis (think about laundry detergent, perfumes, etc.) The safety & toxicity of all of these fragrances aren't really regulated by the government or any organization either. We're overwhelming our noses & respiratory systems with chemical fragrances on a daily basis & then we wonder why people are breaking out in overwhelming numbers with allergies, skin issues, asthma, breathing problems, etc. So I'm all for companies like this one who use only essential oils for their fragrances in their product instead of using unnecessary, unhealthy chemical scents!! :D *THANK YOU!*

And the wood wick is in my opinion a neat, cool effect that my son & I enjoy looking at the candle & listening to! Sure, it doesn't sound like a roaring fire lol, & you aren't going to "hear" it unless you get up close to the candle & listen to it or if you're sitting in quiet room with it, but my son & I thoroughly enjoy the effect of it. It's nice to just sit & listen to it for a few minutes with him & have some peace & quiet enjoying it...teaching my kid that not everything has to be "loud & fun" to enjoy something in life! And if you're quietly reading or using your computer with no tv/music on in the background, you can hear the quiet, relaxing crackling of this candle! :P

The packaging the candle comes in is really, really nice for gift giving too! It comes in an elegant, well made black box with a flip-open lid that easily & conveniently magnetizes shut & is really great for re-using! Inside of it the candle is snugly wrapped in a black velvet bag (my son just loves those types of bags & he uses them as "coin purses" like he's a "pirate" lol!) The glass jar is great for re-using as well & I like that the design on it is simple & classy & isn't a big sticker label like other candles have that wind up looking tacky sometimes if you want to re-use the empty jar for anything when the candle is done with. And then if you wash the jar more than once, those stickers can wind up starting to peel off...but with this jar you don't have that kind of problem! :)

The amber/brown jar also lights up nicely when the candle is lit & I like that it's a pleasing color to men as well, so you can make the man in your life happy to use these candles as well! This company's candles make great gifts not only for women, but also for men, being that they aren't packaged in any kind of "girly" way & many of the scents I think are pleasing to both men & women!

It also came with an additional little candle in a black metal tin. It's "White Sage" scent for you to try (I haven't lit it yet, but it smells like a relaxing, light floral scent.) The lid screws on, making it a secure little tin to hold jewelry in while traveling, etc...once the candle is used up & you wash it out.

Overall, I think this is another one of this company's WONDERFUL candles & it's nicely scented for gift giving or for treating yourself! It really hits all of the boxes for me for what I'd want in a candle...being made in the USA & made of coconut wax & essential oils & having no chemical fragrances, in a nice amber glass jar with no sticker label on it, packaged in a lovely, elegant way with other usable items (the fancy black box & black velvet pouch)...which makes it very suitable for gift giving to a man, not just to a woman...being that it isn't packaged or decorated in a distinctly "feminine" sort of way. And the company makes various "woodsy" scents as well, which any man would be sure to appreciate! And then it also comes with an extra little candle yet too!

I think this is just a superb product that goes even above & beyond your usual "natural candle." So a customer can feel good supporting an American company like this which produces such a quality, well made, nicely-crafted, all-natural, eco-friendly item! And don't forget that they've partnered with a tree-planting organization that plants one tree for every candle of theirs purchased, so any candle you purchase from them also pays for a tree being planted! Which is SO IMPORTANT nowadays with how big a problem deforestation has become! So what isn't there to like about this company & their candles?! Even their wood wicks are from FSC-certified wood mills which means the wood is supposed to be sustainably harvested! :D I wish every company was this conscientious with the products they made!

-IT'S HEAVY. LOTS OF EXTRA ENERGY GOES INTO WALKING WITH THE iWALK, UNTIL YOU ADJUST. I AM A SWEATY PIG ALL THE TIME. LOL. BUT I'M IN SHAPE AND WORKED EXTRA HARD PRIOR TO SURGERY, ANTICIPATING THAT IT WOULD BE TO MY BENEFIT....GOOD THINKING, SELF.
-PART OF GETTING USED TO IT IS LIFTING IT UP A BIT DURING GAIT VS. A NORMAL GAIT PATTERN. THE STUMBLES HAPPENED WHEN I FORGOT TO FOCUS WHILE GETTING USED TO IT THE FIRST 2-3 DAYS OF CONSISTENT USE. I'VE BEEN ON IT FOR 1 WEEK SOLID AND STUMBLE VERY RARELY. I FOUND THAT SHORTENING THE PEG APPROXIMATELY 1/2 INCH FROM THE LENGTH OF MY UNINJURED LEG HELPED WITH STUMBLING....DRAW BACK, MUST BE AWARE OF LEG LENGTH DIFFERENCE AND NOT HABITUATE INTO STRAIGHT LEG STANDING WITH UNINJURED LEG IF YOU'D LIKE TO AVOID LOW BACK DISCOMFORT.

WHAT I LOVE:
-BEING HANDS FREE IS PRICELESS. I SHOPPED FOR A CHRISTMAS TREE, HELPED WITH THANKSGIVING PREP, HELPED DECORATE MY HOME, CARRY MY PUP, VACCUUM, SWEEP AND OTHER LIGHT CHORES. BENDING OVER TO PICK THINGS UP IS EASY ONCE YOU'VE HABITUATED.

WHAT I DON'T LOVE:

STAIRS:

BIG VEHICLES:

ALL-IN-ALL, THOUGH: I'D BUY IT AGAIN! WE ARE INJURED, FOLKS!!! NOTHING IS GOING TO BE AS EASY AS IT WAS PRE-INJURY! THIS IS BY FAR MY FAVORITE ADAPTIVE DEVICE OF THE THREE I OWN (CRUTCHES LASTED ABOUT 5 MIN). SO GIVE IT A SHOT IF YOU FIT THE QUALIFICATIONS LISTED IN THE PRODUCT DESCRIPTION. top_screaming.text: I AM 50YRS OLD, 6'4 SOMEWHAT OVER 300 LBS...I WORK OUTSIDE IN A VERY PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT WALKING ON METAL CONSTANTLY APPROX 12 HRS PER DAY, WEARING STEEL TOED WORK BOOTS.  ABOUT 2 YRS AGO MY RIGHT FOOT JUST IN FRONT OF THE HEEL STARTED HURTING REALLY BAD, WOULD LAY IN BED AND I WOULD GET SHARP PAINS ON BOTTOM OF FOOT, FELT LIKE A NAIL BEING DRIVEN THROUGH MY FOOT, AND IT WAS SPORADIC, NO PAIN THEN ALL OF A SUDDEN SHARP STABBING, THEN IT WOULD LET OFF.  I WENT TO FOOT DR. HE TOLD ME IT WAS PLANTAR FACITIS..HE GAVE ME A SHOT IN MY FOOT AND SOLD ME SOME INSERT/INSOLES FOR MY WORKBOOTS. HELPED FOR A VERY SHORT PERIOD, HE ALSO GAVE ME SOME EXERCISES TO DO TO HELP (THEY DIDN'T)...ANYHOO, LONG STORY KINDA SHORT...FOR THE PAST TWO YEARS I HAVE TRIED JUST ABOUT EVERY TYPE OF INSERT I COULD BUY,,PAID UPWARDS OF 30 DOLLARS FOR SOME...TO NO AVAIL..NOW BOTH OF MY FEET HURT REALLY BAD, CAN HARDLY WALK AT TIMES..I DID SEE THE PAID ADVERTISEMENT SHOW ONE SUNDAY MORN FOR THE WALKFIT PLATINUM..THOUGHT WHAT THE HECK, SO INSTEAD OF BUYING FROM THE ADVERTISER, I LOOKED ON AMAZON AND SURE ENOUGH THEY WERE HERE WITH THE SIZE I NEEDED. VERY REASONABLY PRICED AT THAT.  I ORDERED TWO PAIR, THESE ARE VERY RIGID, WHICH IS WHAT I HAVE BEEN LOOKING FOR, AT MY SIZE ALL THE OTHERS JUST SMASHED OUT, ANYHOO, FIRST DAY, I READ THE INSTRUCTIONS, THE CAME WITH OPTIONS FOR THE TYPE OF ARCH ONE MAY HAVE, I PUT THE HIGH ARCH OPTION ON WAY TOO MUCH SO I WENT TO THE LOWEST. WORE THEM FOR ONE HOUR AS PRESCRIBED BY THE INSTRUCTION, I COULD TELL A DIFFERENCE FROM THE BEGINNING, REMOVE THEM FROM MY SHOES AFTER THE ONE HOUR, FOOT ALREADY FELT BETTER. NOW THEY WERE A LITTLE UNCOMFORTABLE AND WILL TAKE A WHILE TO GET USED TO THEM (AS PER THE INSTRUCTIONS) BUT MY FOOT DID NOT HURT. I HAVE ALSO STARTED USING A TENNIS BALL TO HELP MASSAGE BOTTOM OF FOOT, (I FOUND THAT TRICK THIS AFTERNOON WHILE LOOKING UP WALKFIT ON YOUTUBE) THAT WORKS ALSO, VIDEO ON YOUTUBE ON HOW TO DO THAT.IT'S EASY AND PROVIDES RELIEF..SO TODAY I AM VERY HAPPY, THERE MAY BE LIGHT AT THE END OF THE TUNNEL!..BETWEEN THE WALKFIT AND THE TENNIS BALL MASSAGE MY FEET FEEL BETTER ALREADY!  WILL UPDATE IN A MONTH TO SEE IF I AM STILL EXCITED.

top_screaming.text:
I LOVE IT BECAUSE I HATE MOPPING USING A BUCKET, THIS IS SO MUSH EASIER. I'LL CLEAN MY KITCHEN FLOOR MORE OFTEN.!!

THE REASON I'M WRITING THIS IS BECAUSE I COULDN'T FIND EASY INSTRUCTIONS TO FOLLOW LIKE THIS ANYWHERE, INCLUDING THEIR INSTRUCTIONS OR THE INTERNET!

I HATE MOPPING SO MUCH THAT I RARELY DID IT!
I SAW AN ADVERTISMENT FOR SWIFTER WET JET ON TV AND DECIDED TO TRY IT.

I GOT IT DELIVERED FROM THE GROCERY STORE FULLY ASSEMBLED.

THESE ARE THE STEPS I TOOK BEFORE USING IT:

1. REMOVE THE WRAP FROM HANDLE, PULL HANDLE UP BUT NOT PULLING TOO HARD, UNTIL YOU SNAP IT IN PLACE. IF YOU HAVE PULLED IT TOO HARD AND TOP OF HANDLE COMES OUT, JUST PUSH IN BUTTON ON HANDLE AND GENTELY SLIP DOWN UNTIL YOU FIND THE HOLE IT SNAPS INTO. CUT THE HEAVY CELLOPANE FROM THE REST OF MOP.

2. FIRST OFF, WHERE ARE THE INSTUCTIONS AND THE SCUBING PAD AS SEEN IN PICTURE? THEY'RE IN BOX WITH CLEANING PADS! AND YOU HAVE TO PULL OUT BOTH PADS FOR THE STRIP AND INSTRUCTIONS TO COME OUT.

WHERE DOES THE SCRUBING STRIP GO? ON FRONT ON MOP HEAD THERE IS A VELRO STRIP. JUST PUSH STRIP ONTO IT

2. PUT IN BATTERIES PUSH THE DOOR (LOCATED JUST ABOVE WHERE THE CLEANING SOLVENT GOES,) PUSH IN AND PULL UP AND OUT. PUT IN BATTERIES AND TO CLOSE DOOR PUSH IN AND DOWN UNTIL IT CLICKS INTO PLACE

3. REMOVE STROFOAM AROUND CLEANING SOVLENT AND FIT BOTTLE OF CLEANING SOLVENT INTO PLACE BY PRESSING DOWN THE BOTTLE WITH THE CAP FACING DOWN AND THE LABEL SIDE FACING OUT. MAKE SURE IT'S SECURLY IN PLACE

4. PUT CLEANING PAD ON BOTTON OF MOP HEAD WITH THE SMOOTH SIDE FACING THE MOP AND THE CLOTH LIKE SIDE FACING DDWN.

5. NOW IT'S TIME TO START, JUST PUSH THE BUTTON ON HANDLE AND SPRAY WILL COME OUT, PUSH YUUR MOP BACK AND FORTH. IF THE DIRTY AREA IS TOO HARD FOR JUST THE SOLVENT IN MOP TO GET OUT. USE A HEAVY DUTY CLEANER FULL STRENTH. I HADN'T MOPPED IN AGES (I HATE DOING IT). BUT I HAD "SIMPLE GREEN" ON HAND, AND IF ANYBODY HAS USED IT KNOWS HOW STRONG IT IS.

SIMPLY SPRAY SOME SIMPLE GREEN ON STAIN AND LET IT SET FOR A FEW MINUTES. AND THEN RUB WITH MOP SCRUBING DOWN

IF THIS IS THE FIRST TIME AND THE DIRT REFUSES TO BREAK DOWN, USE SIMPLE GREEN AND GET DOWN ON YOUR KNEES AND SCRUB WITH SCOURING PAD.

NOW THAT I HAVE A MOP THAT'S EASY TO USE AND REQUIRES NO BUCKET OF WATER, I'LL MOP EVERY WEEK!!

I BUY PACKAGES OF SCOURING PADS AND USE SOME FOR CLEANING POTS AND PANS AND THE OTHERS FOR THESE KINDS OF CLEANING USES.

These 7 gallon trash bags are the perfect size for the small trash cans we have throughout the house in the bathrooms, bedrooms, and in my home office. top_five_star_obscene.text: As long as you know what to expect going in, you should be pleased with these biodegradable small garbage bags. They are thin but fairly strong. They will stand up to some stretching, but aren’t going to handle anything sharp. I’ve used many biodegradable garbage bags and like all the others, these will start to break down within a week if you put anything wet in them. We have larger ones we use in the kitchen garbage, and while we compost most of our food waste, sometimes something wet gets in the bag. Since it’s not uncommon for the same bag to be in the garbage can for a week and not emptied until trash pickup day, it’s also not uncommon for some leakage by the end of the week. The same is the case with these. They are better suited for dry garbage, or as indicated as their purpose, for compostables that you will empty frequently. That said, I’m willing to put up with some inconvenience in order to keep plastic garbage bag use at a minimum in our house, so I’m happy with these as they help achieve that goal.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Good quality, but know what to expect!
